incidents.vector: Exploited CVE-2026-20131, a critical zero-day in Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center, weeks before Cisco's March 4 disclosure.
incidents.impact: Interlock deployed custom RATs, JavaScript backdoors, and memory-resident implants across victim networks. Past victims include St. Paul, MN government and DaVita dialysis centers.

incidents.vector: CVE-2026-22557, a CVSS 10 unauthenticated path-traversal vulnerability in Ubiquiti UniFi Network Application, disclosed with 88,000 hosts publicly exposed.
incidents.impact: No exploitation confirmed yet, but trivial to automate once endpoints identified. Roughly 33% of exposed instances are in the United States.

incidents.vector: Phishing email exploiting CVE-2025-66376 (XSS in Zimbra webmail), with malicious code embedded directly in the email HTML body — no attachments or links required.
incidents.impact: Attackers harvested login credentials, session tokens, backup 2FA codes, browser-stored passwords, and up to 90 days of mailbox data from the maritime navigation agency.

incidents.victim: IoT device owners (3 million routers and cameras compromised)
incidents.vector: Four botnets (Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid, Mossad) compromised over 3 million IoT devices and launched 300,000+ DDoS attack commands demanding extortion payments.
incidents.impact: DOJ with Canadian and German authorities dismantled botnet infrastructure. Victims reported tens of thousands in losses; some U.S. Department of Defense systems were targeted.

incidents.vector: Nine vulnerabilities disclosed across Cisco SD-WAN and firewall products over three weeks, with five already exploited in the wild — two SD-WAN zero-days exploited for over three years before discovery.
incidents.impact: Compromise of network management and control planes gives attackers control over policy, routing, segmentation, and administrative trust across entire environments.

incidents.victim: Mobile device users in Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Malaysia
incidents.attacker: Unknown — multiple state-linked espionage groups and commercial surveillance vendors
incidents.vector: DarkSword, a full-chain iOS exploit leveraging six zero-day vulnerabilities in iOS 18.4–18.7, deployed via Snapchat-themed phishing sites and watering hole attacks.
incidents.impact: Post-compromise malware exfiltrates accounts, messages, browser data, location history, and recordings. Deployed by at least three distinct groups including a Turkish surveillance vendor and a Russian espionage unit. Patched in iOS 26.3.

incidents.vector: Medusa ransomware encrypted hospital systems, forcing Mississippi's only children's hospital, Level I trauma center, and organ transplant program to operate with analog tools for nine days.
incidents.impact: Cancer infusion patients rescheduled, FBI and DHS deployed to assist recovery. Ransom demand of $800,000 with threat to leak stolen data. Hospital fully reopened March 2.

incidents.vector: Medusa ransomware deployed across county government systems serving nearly 600,000 residents.
incidents.impact: Phone lines and IT systems taken down across government offices, disrupting public services.

incidents.vector: SEO poisoning redirected users searching for legitimate VPN software (Pulse, Fortinet, Ivanti) to spoofed download sites hosting fake MSI installers that side-loaded the Hyrax infostealer.
incidents.impact: Stolen VPN credentials and configuration data exfiltrated to attacker C2, granting access to corporate networks with zero user indication of compromise.

incidents.vector: Attacker impersonated IT support on Microsoft Teams, tricked employee into granting remote access via Quick Assist, then deployed malicious MSI package with DLL side-loading.
incidents.impact: C2 established, credential harvesting and session hijacking tools deployed. DART contained the intrusion before persistence was established.

incidents.vector: Suspected China-based espionage operation targeting military organizations in Southeast Asia, under active investigation by Unit 42.
incidents.impact: Details limited due to ongoing investigation. Campaign targets defense and military infrastructure in the region.

incidents.victim: SocksEscort residential proxy customers and victims
incidents.vector: SocksEscort infected 369,000 home and small-business routers with malware, selling unauthorized IP access to criminals who used it to mask bank account takeovers and cryptocurrency theft.
incidents.impact: DOJ-led international takedown. Specific losses include $1M cryptocurrency theft (NY), $700K wire fraud (PA), and $100K military credit card fraud.

incidents.vector: Compromised Stryker's Microsoft Intune deployment and used legitimate remote-wipe commands to erase 200,000+ devices across 79 countries — no traditional malware deployed.
incidents.impact: Global operations shut down for 9+ days; 5,000 Ireland workers sent home; personal devices wiped. DOJ seized four Handala domains; State Department offered $10M reward. Broader MOIS campaign included threats against IDF personnel and Jewish communities.

incidents.vector: Used modified Mandiant AuraInspector tool to scan for public-facing Salesforce Experience Cloud sites with overly permissive guest user configurations, querying CRM objects without authentication.
incidents.impact: Third widespread attack spree against Salesforce customers in six months. Prior campaigns compromised 200+ instances via Gainsight integrations and 700+ via Salesloft Drift AI agents.

incidents.vector: Exploited CVE-2026-1603, an authentication bypass in Ivanti Endpoint Manager, reportedly since mid-February 2026.
incidents.impact: CISA issued emergency patching directive to federal agencies. Ivanti products have been repeatedly targeted by Chinese state actors, following a pattern of edge-device exploitation.

incidents.victim: Applications using pac4j Java authentication library
incidents.vector: CVE-2026-29000, a CVSS 10 logic flaw in pac4j's JWT/JWE handling, allows authentication bypass using only the server's public RSA key — no secrets needed.
incidents.impact: Downstream impact to Spring Security, Play Framework, Vert.x, and hundreds of other packages. Public proof-of-concept published; many organizations unaware of transitive dependency on pac4j-jwt.
